8.note: You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth — the geographic expansion of the Servant mission from the center (Jerusalem) outward; Acts traces this exact route
His greatness shall reach to the ends of the earth — the messianic shepherd's dominion reaching universally; the mission-mandate of Acts 1:8 enacts this geographic universalism

Ask of me and I will make the nations your inheritance, the ends of the earth your possession — the messianic king receives the nations; the Spirit-empowered witnesses carry the news to those nations.

9.note: A cloud took him out of their sight — the ascension cloud echoes the Danielic cloud of divine presence on which the Son of Man comes to receive dominion; the ascension is the enthronement

The cloud covered the tent of meeting and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle — the cloud that receives Jesus echoes the divine-presence cloud. Where the cloud went, the presence went; the ascension-cloud signals that Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary.

2.note: Elijah was taken up to heaven by a whirlwind while still instructing Elisha — the ascending prophet-figure who commissions a successor is the structural type for Christ's ascension while still teaching his apostles. Elisha receives a double portion of the Spirit; the apostles receive the promised Spirit at Pentecost.

3.note: Moses remained on Sinai forty days and forty nights receiving the Law — the forty-day pattern recurs at every major covenantal moment: Moses at Sinai (Exod 34:28), Elijah at Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8), Jesus in the wilderness (Matt 4:2), now Christ's forty-day resurrection appearances. The number marks a complete period of divine preparation before a new covenant stage.

4.note: I will put my Spirit within you — the promise of the Mosaic-Spirit indwelling the covenant community was foretold by Ezekiel as the mark of the new covenant; 'the gift my Father promised' is the same promise.
I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh — the promise Jesus refers to is Joel's eschatological Spirit-outpouring; its fulfillment at Pentecost is only days away.

5.note: I will pour water on the thirsty land and streams on the dry ground; I will pour my Spirit on your offspring — the water-Spirit metaphor of Isaiah 44 anticipates the baptism-with-Spirit distinction Jesus draws: John baptized with water, but the age of Spirit-saturation is now arriving.

6.note: In that day I will restore David's fallen tent — the disciples' question about restoring the kingdom to Israel reflects the prophetic expectation of national restoration; Amos 9:11 and Ezek 37:21–22 frame the hope. Christ's answer deflects the timeline but does not deny the promise.

7.note: He changes times and seasons; he deposes kings and raises up others — Daniel's statement about God's sovereignty over times is the background to Jesus's reply that the Father has set the times and dates by his own authority. The times of the end are in the same divine hands that directed all prior history.

11.note: On that day his feet will stand on the Mount of Olives — the promised return from heaven to the same mountain establishes continuity between the ascension and the parousia. The angels' announcement anticipates the Zechariah prophecy of the Lord's return to the place from which he departed.

12.note: The Mount of Olives, which is before Jerusalem on the east — Luke's geographic note connects the ascension site to the eschatological return-site of Zechariah 14:4, where the Lord will stand on the Mount of Olives in the day of his coming.

16.note: Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me — Ps 41:9 underlies the 'Scripture had to be fulfilled' concerning Judas's betrayal; the psalm of the one abandoned by a trusted companion is applied to the betrayal within the Twelve.

18.note: They weighed out thirty pieces of silver... I threw them to the potter — Zechariah's thirty silver coins thrown to the potter in the temple yard provide the background for the Judas narrative; Matthew 27:3–10 cites this explicitly, and Acts's field-purchase reflects the same event. The connection is to the purchase of the field.

20.note: May his place be deserted; let there be no one to dwell in it — Peter cites Ps 69:25 as the scriptural basis for Judas's vacant place; the psalm of the righteous sufferer who is abandoned by his enemies is applied to the abandonment of Judas's position.

May another take his place of leadership — Peter's second citation (Ps 109:8) establishes the scriptural warrant for replacing Judas; the psalm of the king abandoned by treacherous allies is used to authorize the selection of a twelfth apostle.

26.note: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD — casting lots to determine Matthias's election follows the OT practice of using lots as a means of discerning divine choice (Num 26:55; Josh 7:14). The lot is not random chance but a covenantal decision-making instrument.

17.note: And in the last days, God declares, I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh — Peter cites Joel 2:28-32 as the explanation for Pentecost; the outpouring of the Spirit on all flesh (sons, daughters, young, old, male and female servants) is the eschatological fulfillment of the promise

25.note: I saw the Lord always before me — David's Psalm of trust (you will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor let your Holy One see corruption) cited by Peter as resurrection prophecy; David spoke of the Christ, not himself

34.note: The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool — Peter cites Ps 110:1 as the proof that God has made Jesus both Lord and Christ (v.36); the ascension-and-enthronement as the Psalm's fulfillment

1.note: On the first day of the third month the Israelites arrived at Sinai — Pentecost (Shavuot) falls fifty days after Passover, the same timing as Israel's arrival at Sinai. Jewish tradition by the first century associated Shavuot with the giving of the Law. The Spirit is given at the feast that commemorates Torah-giving: the new Torah (the Spirit who writes on hearts, Jer 31:33) arrives at the appointed time.

2.note: Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe into these slain — the violent wind from heaven recalls Ezekiel's wind-breath that restores the valley of dry bones. Pentecost is the enactment of Ezekiel's vision: a spiritually dead people receive new life through the divine breath/Spirit.

Mount Sinai was covered with smoke, because the LORD descended on it in fire — the Sinai theophany combines fire and dramatic sound; the Pentecost sound from heaven and subsequent fire on heads echoes the Sinai theophany in which God descended to give the covenant.

3.note: Tongues of fire separating and resting on each of them — fire is consistently the mark of divine presence in the OT (burning bush: Exod 3:2; Sinai: Exod 19:18; Ezek 1:13). The individual distribution of the fire to each disciple signals that the divine presence now rests directly on each person, not only on the sanctuary.

4.note: Would that all the LORD's people were prophets, and that the LORD would put his Spirit on them — Moses's wish in the wilderness is fulfilled at Pentecost: the Spirit falls on all who are present, and they all prophesy. What Moses desired is what Peter will call 'what was spoken by the prophet Joel.'

5.note: Three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD your God — Shavuot (Pentecost) was one of the three pilgrimage feasts; God-fearing Jews from every nation had gathered to Jerusalem precisely because the feast law drew diaspora Jews back to the holy city. The geographic scattering that created this international audience is itself the reversal-in-process of the Babel diaspora.

6.note: The crowd hears in their own language — Babel is the OT type; Pentecost is the antitype that reverses it. At Babel, one language was divided into many tongues and the peoples were scattered; at Pentecost, the Spirit enables the diverse tongues of the nations to hear the gospel in their own language. The curse of linguistic division is reversed in the Spirit's work.

11.note: I am coming to gather all nations and tongues, and they shall come and see my glory — the Pentecost gathering of representatives from every nation is the first installment of the eschatological ingathering that Isaiah 66 anticipated. The nations are not just present but understand the 'wonders of God' in their own tongues.

18.note: Even on my servants, both men and women, I will pour out my Spirit in those days — the continuation of the Joel citation emphasizes the democratization of prophetic access: no distinction of class, gender, or age. The Spirit breaks the restriction of prophecy to designated individuals that characterized the Old Covenant era.

19.note: I will show wonders in the heavens and signs on the earth — the cosmic portents in Joel's vision frame the day of Pentecost as the beginning of the eschatological era between the first and second comings of Christ, a period defined by prophetic outpouring and anticipating cosmic renewal.

20.note: The sun will be turned to darkness and the moon to blood before the coming of the great and glorious day of the Lord — the Joel citation places Pentecost within the eschatological arc that culminates in 'the day of the Lord'; the Spirit's coming is not the end but the beginning of the end-time sequence.

21.note: And everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved — the climax of Peter's Joel citation: the promise of universal salvation-access through calling on the Lord's name. The invitation of v.38 ('repent and be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ') is the application of this promise.

Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth — Isaiah's universal salvation-invitation is the prophetic register in which Joel 2:32 operates; Peter's use of Joel 2:32 carries the same universalist logic: salvation is available to all who call.

22.note: If what a prophet proclaims in the name of the LORD does not take place, that is a message the LORD has not spoken — the miracles, wonders, and signs that accredited Jesus are precisely the prophetic-authentication criteria Moses specified. Jesus was accredited by God through signs that demonstrated divine authorization.

23.note: You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good — the deliberate-plan logic of the crucifixion (handed over by God's foreknowledge; executed by wicked men) is structurally identical to the Joseph story: human sin and divine purpose operating simultaneously. The cross is not an accident of history but God's foreordained act accomplished through human agency.

Yet it was the LORD's will to crush him and cause him to suffer — the 'deliberate plan and foreknowledge' of God behind the crucifixion enacts the Servant Song's insistence that the Servant's suffering was not random but divinely appointed for the bearing of sin.

24.note: The cords of death entangled me — 'freeing him from the agony of death' uses the same imagery as Ps 18:4-5 (the cords/snares of death). The resurrection is God releasing the righteous one from death's grip, which is exactly the deliverance-pattern the Psalms describe.

26.note: Therefore my heart is glad and my tongue rejoices; my body also will rest in hope — the continuation of Peter's Ps 16 citation; the 'resting in hope' is the confident expectation of bodily resurrection, not merely spiritual immortality, as v.31 will make explicit.

27.note: You will not abandon me to the realm of the dead, nor will you let your Holy One see decay — the resurrection-prediction at the center of Peter's argument: Ps 16:10 identifies the Christ as the Holy One whose body does not see corruption. Since David's body did see corruption, Peter argues David was prophesying about his descendant.

28.note: You will fill me with joy in your presence — the path of life (v.28 = Ps 16:11) is the resurrection life into which the Father brings the Son; the joy of the divine presence is the condition of the risen, ascended Christ now at the right hand.

30.note: The LORD swore an oath to David: One of your own descendants I will place on your throne — Peter cites the Davidic covenant oath as the prophetic ground for Christ's resurrection-enthronement; 2 Sam 7:12-16 is the background, and Ps 132:11 is the liturgical memorial of the oath.

I will raise up your offspring to succeed you... I will establish the throne of his kingdom forever — the Davidic covenant's promise of an eternal throne is fulfilled not in Solomon (whose throne fell) but in the resurrection-enthronement of Christ, whose throne at the right hand of God is permanent.

33.note: Exalted to the right hand of God — the ascension-enthronement fulfills Ps 110:1 ('sit at my right hand'), which Peter will cite explicitly in vv.34-35. The Spirit poured out (v.33b) is the evidence that the ascension has occurred and the Psalm's enthronement is operative.

36.note: The kings of the earth set themselves against the LORD and against his Anointed (Messiah) — 'God has made this Jesus both Lord and Messiah' declares the fulfillment of Ps 2's royal-enthronement scenario; the one plotted against is now installed as Lord and Messiah through the resurrection.

37.note: They will look on the one they have pierced, and they will mourn — the crowd being 'cut to the heart' enacts the Zechariah vision of Israel mourning the one they pierced; the penitential response to Peter's proclamation is the beginning of Zech 12:10's fulfillment, though its full scope reaches the parousia.

38.note: I will forgive their wickedness and remember their sins no more — the offer of forgiveness in the name of Jesus Christ is the enactment of the new covenant promise of Jer 31:34; the baptism-for-forgiveness is the covenant rite that grants entry into the community for whom sins are forgotten.

39.note: Peace, peace, to the far and the near — 'the promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off' echoes Isa 57:19's universal peace-offer. The 'far off' will become a key phrase for Gentile inclusion (Eph 2:13, 17), but here it may still refer to diaspora Jews.

40.note: They are corrupt and not his children; they are a warped and crooked generation — Moses's Song warns Israel about the consequences of apostasy; Peter's 'save yourselves from this corrupt generation' applies the same Deuteronomic idiom to the generation that crucified Jesus.

41.note: About three thousand of the people fell that day — at Sinai, three thousand were killed for the golden calf idolatry on the day the Law was given; at Pentecost, three thousand are added to the new covenant community on the day the Spirit is given. The contrast is deliberate: where law brought death, the Spirit gives life (2 Cor 3:6).

42.note: They saw God, and they ate and drank — the covenantal meal before God at Sinai is the OT precedent for the communal table fellowship of the new covenant community; the breaking of bread together is the continuation of the Passover-Last Supper covenant meal in which Christ is host.

44.note: There need be no poor people among you, for in the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess as your inheritance, he will richly bless you — the Deuteronomic ideal of a community with no poor is enacted in the Jerusalem church's sharing of possessions. The community of the new covenant lives out what the Jubilee vision aimed at.

46.note: Suddenly the Lord you are seeking will come to his temple — the daily temple presence of the new community fulfills the Malachi promise that the Lord would suddenly come to his temple; the disciples meet in the temple courts because they are the community of the Lord who has come.

13.note: The God of our fathers glorified his servant Jesus — Peter identifies Jesus as the Servant whose exaltation Isa 52:13-53:12 predicted; the glorification of the Servant is the resurrection/ascension
The God of Abraham and Isaac and Jacob — the burning bush self-identification; Peter anchors the resurrection-claim in the covenant God who is always the God of the living

22.note: Moses said: The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers — Peter cites the Mosaic prophet-promise as fulfilled in Jesus; the specific prophet-like-Moses that Stephen will also cite (7:37)

25.note: In your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed — Peter cites the Abrahamic promise as fulfilled through Jesus; the blessing to all nations is the mission of Acts

All peoples on earth will be blessed through you — the original Abrahamic call promise is the foundation; Peter identifies the audience as 'heirs of the prophets and the covenant,' making the point that Christ's work is not a departure from the Abrahamic covenant but its fulfillment.

1.note: Evening, morning and noon I cry out in distress, and he hears my voice — the three daily hours of prayer (including the ninth hour / 3pm) reflect the Jewish prayer practice codified from the Psalms and observed by Daniel (Dan 6:10). The apostles continue in Israel's prayer rhythm as they go to the temple.

2.note: Then the lame will leap like a deer — Isaiah's vision of the messianic age includes the lame leaping (Isa 35:6); the lame man at the temple gate is the sign that the age of salvation has arrived. Peter's healing is the continuation of Jesus's ministry (Matt 11:5 cites the same Isa 35 sign to answer John's question).

6.note: Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering — the healing in the name of Jesus Christ extends the Servant's vicarious bearing of infirmities (Isa 53:4–5; Matt 8:17) through his apostles; the healing power operates by invoking the name of the one who bore the disease.

8.note: He jumped to his feet and began to walk, leaping and praising God — the lame man's leaping directly fulfills Isa 35:6 ('the lame will leap like a deer'). Luke's language matches the Isaianic description closely, signaling that the messianic healing Isaiah predicted is now occurring in Jesus's name.

14.note: The Holy and Righteous One — Peter's titles for Jesus echo Isa 53:11 ('the righteous one, my servant, will justify many') and Ps 16:10 ('your Holy One'). Requesting a murderer's release in exchange for the Holy and Righteous One is the paradox that the Servant Song predicted: the innocent one condemned while the guilty go free.

15.note: You killed the author of life, but God raised him from the dead — 'after he has suffered he will see the light of life' (Isa 53:10-11); the one killed is paradoxically the source of life, which the resurrection vindicates. The title archēgon (author/pioneer/source of life) carries the OT background of the one through whom all life comes.

17.note: I know that you acted in ignorance — the Levitical law made provision for sins committed in ignorance (Lev 4:2; Num 15:22-29), which is distinct from deliberate, high-handed sin. Peter's appeal to ignorance is not exculpatory but mitigating — it opens the door to the repentance-and-forgiveness that follows in vv.19–20.

18.note: God fulfilled what all the prophets had foretold: that his Messiah would suffer — Peter claims that the suffering Messiah is not a theological novelty but the unanimous prophetic witness, of which Isa 53 is the most developed expression. The death of Christ is the accomplishment of the entire prophetic trajectory.

The Messiah would suffer — Ps 22 is the suffering-unto-vindication psalm cited from the cross (Matt 27:46); its arc of abandonment and ultimate deliverance is one of the prophetic texts behind Peter's claim that all the prophets foretold the Messiah's suffering.

19.note: Times of refreshing may come from the Lord — Isaiah's invitation to the thirsty to come and eat freely is the OT register of 'times of refreshing'; the repentance Peter calls for is the turning to receive what God freely offers in the age of the Messiah.

20.note: He may send the Messiah, who has been appointed for you — Malachi's promise that God would send his messenger and then the Lord himself would suddenly come is fulfilled in stages; Peter's language of the appointed Messiah being sent again points to the parousia as the final fulfillment of Malachi's promised coming.

21.note: God will restore everything, as he promised through his holy prophets — the 'restoration of all things' (apokatastasis) is the OT prophetic horizon of Isa 65:17-25 (new creation), Ezek 36:28-35, and Acts 1:6's question about restoring the kingdom. Christ's return will accomplish what the prophets foresaw.

Elijah to restore all things before the great and dreadful day of the LORD — 'restore everything' carries Malachi's eschatological restoration language; the prophets anticipated a final act of divine renewal before the day of the Lord, which the ascended Christ will accomplish at his return.

23.note: Anyone who does not listen to him will be completely cut off from their people — the stern consequence Peter attaches to rejecting Jesus echoes Deut 18:19 and Lev 23:29; the prophet-like-Moses must be heeded or the hearer is cut off. This is the urgency beneath the call to repentance.

26.note: When God raised up his servant, he sent him first to you — the Servant's mission begins in Israel and extends outward (Isa 49:6); Peter's 'sent him first to you' is the first stage of the Servant's universal mission. The blessings of the resurrection are offered first to Israel in the sequence that also includes 'the ends of the earth.'

11.note: This Jesus is the stone that was rejected by you, the builders, which has become the cornerstone — Peter applies Ps 118:22 to the resurrection: the crucified-and-risen Jesus is the rejected stone now exalted as cornerstone of the new community

25.note: Why did the Gentiles rage and the peoples plot in vain? The kings of the earth set themselves and the rulers were gathered together against the Lord and against his Anointed — the Jerusalem church cites Ps 2 as fulfilled in the coalition against Jesus at the passion; the nations' rage against the Anointed is the historical crucifixion

The prayer quotes Ps 2:1-2 verbatim (LXX): 'Why do the nations rage and the peoples plot in vain? The kings of the earth rise up and the rulers band together against the LORD and against his anointed one.' The royal psalm's conspiring rulers are now identified with the specific historical agents — Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and Israel — who cooperated in the crucifixion.

2.note: The resurrection of Jesus proclaimed here is grounded in Ps 16:10 ('you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor let your holy one see corruption') — the proof-text Peter deployed in Acts 2:27 that the Sadducees found intolerable, since Sadducees denied resurrection entirely.
Isaiah's promise that the dead will rise and their bodies will be raised echoes through the apostolic resurrection proclamation — the eschatological hope the Jerusalem establishment rejected.

8.note: The Spirit placed in the mouth of God's servant so that words will not depart — Peter's Spirit-filled speech before the rulers fulfills the prophetic pattern of the anointed speaker given words by the Spirit in moments of judicial pressure.

God's promise to Moses before Pharaoh ('I will be with your mouth and teach you what to say') — the apostles' Spirit-enabled speech before the council parallels the pattern of the exodus confrontation with power.

12.note: Joel's promise that 'everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved' provides the theological ground for Peter's claim that salvation is found in no other name — the LORD whose name saves in Joel is now identified with the name of Jesus.

YHWH's declaration that there is no other God and no other Savior ('there is no God apart from me, a righteous God and a Savior; there is none but me') stands behind the exclusivity claim — the God who alone saves in Isaiah is now revealed to save through Jesus's name.

13.note: Amos's self-identification as 'not a prophet nor a prophet's son — I was a shepherd' is the prototype for the pattern of God choosing unschooled outsiders to be his spokesmen; the council's astonishment at unlearned men speaking with authority echoes the repeated biblical pattern of unauthorized-but-empowered messengers.

20.note: Jeremiah's confession — 'his word is in my heart like a fire, a fire shut up in my bones; I am weary of holding it in, indeed I cannot' — is the OT prototype for the apostles' declaration that they cannot stop speaking what they have seen and heard; the prophetic compulsion drives the testimony.

24.note: The community's prayer opening ('Sovereign Lord, who made the heavens and the earth and the sea and everything in them') echoes the Decalogue's Sabbath rationale — the appeal to YHWH as Creator grounds the prayer in the covenant identity of the God they serve.

Hezekiah's prayer before Sennacherib opens identically ('LORD Almighty, God of Israel, enthroned between the cherubim, you alone are God over all the kingdoms of the earth; you have made heaven and earth') — the early community patterns their crisis-prayer on the great OT model of covenant appeal to the Creator-God.

27.note: The prayer explicitly interprets Ps 2's conspiring kings and rulers as fulfilled in Herod (king of the earth) and Pontius Pilate (ruler), with the Gentiles and Israel as the raging nations — the first-century passion is read as the eschatological fulfillment of the anointed king's enemies gathering against him.

28.note: God's declaration that he announces the end from the beginning and accomplishes his purpose — the prayer's affirmation that the conspiring rulers 'did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen' grounds the passion in the divine sovereignty that Isaiah proclaimed over history.

31.note: The shaking of the place at the Spirit's filling echoes Sinai's shaking when the LORD descended in fire — the new covenant community experiences the same theophanic trembling that marked the original covenant inauguration.

The doorposts and thresholds shaking at the divine presence in Isaiah's temple vision — the prayer-meeting shaking signals the same kind of intensive divine presence that Isaiah experienced at his commissioning.

32.note: God's promise in Deuteronomy — 'there need be no poor people among you' — if the covenant community practices the Jubilee-shaped generosity of debt release and sharing; the community of 4:32-35 is the enacted fulfillment of what Deuteronomy set as the ideal of covenant social life.

35.note: The Levitical command to support the poor Israelite whose resources fail — the communal pooling of property and distribution according to need enacts the covenant economic principles of Leviticus rather than the Qumran model of obligatory surrender; it is voluntary covenant sharing.

37.note: This is the Moses who said to the Israelites: God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers — Stephen's speech culminates in the Moses-like-prophet typology; Jesus is the definitive prophet that Moses prefigured

42.note: Did you bring me sacrifices and offerings during the forty years in the wilderness? — Stephen cites Amos 5:25-27 for Israel's wilderness idolatry; the pattern of Israel rejecting YHWH's prophets culminates in their rejection of Jesus
You took up the tent of Moloch and the star of your god Rephan — the citation of Amos's wilderness-idolatry charge as the pattern of Israel's consistent rejection of the Spirit (v.51)

49.note: Heaven is my throne and the earth is my footstool — Stephen cites Isa 66:1-2 in his critique of the temple; the Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands; the temple is not the ultimate dwelling of the transcendent God

32.note: Like a sheep he was led to the slaughter — Philip reads the Ethiopian eunuch Isa 53:7-8 (the silent suffering lamb) and proclaims Jesus as its fulfillment; the first recorded post-resurrection missionary exposition of the Servant Song

33.note: You are my Son, today I have begotten you — Paul at Pisidian Antioch cites Ps 2:7 as fulfilled in the resurrection; the begetting of the Son in Ps 2 is the resurrection-exaltation that God has fulfilled for Israel's children in raising Jesus

34.note: I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David — Paul cites Isa 55:3 as fulfilled through the resurrection; the covenant mercies promised to David are now available through the risen Christ

35.note: You will not let your Holy One see corruption — Paul cites Ps 16:10 (as Peter did in Acts 2:27) as proof that David's Psalm prophesied the resurrection of Christ who did not see corruption

47.note: I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth — Paul cites Isa 49:6 as his authority for turning to the Gentiles; the Servant's mission to the nations is the warrant for the Gentile mission

16.note: After this I will return and rebuild the tent of David — James cites Amos 9:11-12 at the Jerusalem Council as the scriptural basis for Gentile inclusion without circumcision; the rebuilding of David's fallen tent is the resurrection of Jesus and the formation of the new covenant community that includes all peoples

28.note: In him we live and move and have our being — Paul quotes Greek poets (Epimenides, Aratus) at the Areopagus while making a creation-theology argument from Gen 1-2; all humans are God's offspring, made in his image, dependent on him for existence

22.note: Saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass — Paul's testimony before Agrippa: everything he preaches is the fulfillment of the OT; the Christ who suffers and rises is the culmination of the entire prophetic tradition, not a departure from it

26.note: Go to this people and say: You will indeed hear but never understand — Paul's final citation of Isa 6:9-10 to the Roman Jewish leaders who reject the gospel; the same hardening-text that Jesus cited for his parable-ministry and Matthew cited for Israel's rejection now closes Acts: the Isaianic judgment is the frame for the entire mission

1.note: Achan's act at Jericho — taking from the devoted things (herem) and hiding them while the community consecrated its firstfruits to God — structurally anticipates the Ananias episode: individual deception within a community consecrating its possessions to God brings divine judgment. In both cases the individual's hidden act immediately affects the whole community's standing before God.

5.note: As Achan and his household were destroyed for concealing consecrated goods, so Ananias falls under judgment — the pattern is not merely parallel but theologically continuous: God's holiness within his covenant community cannot accommodate deception about consecrated things; the judgment signals the seriousness of the Spirit's presence.

9.note: Isaiah's warning that Israel 'rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit' — Sapphira's act is classified by Peter as 'testing the Spirit of the Lord,' placing it in the line of Israel's wilderness rebellion against the Spirit's guidance, which resulted in divine opposition to the nation.

12.note: Solomon's Colonnade on the eastern side of the Temple courts was the covered portico where the community gathered — the same location where Jesus walked and taught (John 10:23); the early community's regular assembly in this space continues the pattern of covenant community gathering in the sacred precincts.

15.note: Even Elisha's bones could revive the dead (a corpse thrown into his grave revived on contact) — the account of healing through Peter's shadow belongs to the same biblical pattern of miraculous power concentrated in God's servants that persisted even in their absence or death.

Dwelling in the shadow of the Almighty as protection and refuge — the healing through Peter's shadow ironically inverts the Psalm's image: the divine protective shadow is now mediated through the Spirit-filled apostle, making the servant's shadow the vehicle of the Almighty's healing.

19.note: The angel of the LORD encamping around those who fear God and delivering them — the angel who opens the prison doors fulfills the Psalm's promise of divine protection for the covenant community; the apostles are the 'those who fear him' for whom the angel acts.

In Israel's distress it was no messenger or angel but the Angel of God's Presence who saved them — the apostles' miraculous release echoes the pattern of the angel-of-presence rescue that characterized the Exodus, now applied to the new community under judgment pressure.

29.note: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego before Nebuchadnezzar: 'We will not serve your gods or worship the image of gold' — the apostles' declaration that they must obey God rather than men reactivates the same principle of higher-authority obedience that the Daniel tradition established as the pattern for the faithful in the face of state coercion.

The Hebrew midwives who feared God and did not do what the king commanded — the apostles' disobedience to the Sanhedrin stands in the same line of biblical civil disobedience grounded in the prior claim of divine authority over human command.

30.note: The Deuteronomic law that a man hung on a tree is under God's curse — Peter's description of the crucifixion as 'hanging him on a tree' deliberately activates the curse-language of Deut 21:22-23; the resurrection then means God has reversed the curse, vindicating the one who appeared accursed. Paul makes the Gal 3:13 connection explicit; Peter's language here implies it.

31.note: The LORD's invitation to David's Lord to sit at his right hand — 'God exalted him to his own right hand' applies the Messianic Psalm to the ascended Jesus, confirming that the divine enthronement Ps 110 describes has been realized in the resurrection and exaltation.

Daniel's prophecy of the 'Anointed One, the ruler' (Messiah Prince) — Peter's title 'Prince and Savior' (archēgon kai sōtēra) draws on this Danielic category, presenting the exalted Jesus as the fulfillment of Daniel's anointed ruler.

32.note: God's promise to pour out his Spirit on Israel's offspring — the Spirit given to those who obey is the fulfillment of Isaiah's eschatological Spirit-outpouring; the apostles' witness and the Spirit's witness together constitute the double testimony that Joel 2 promised.

36.note: The Deuteronomic criterion for identifying a false prophet or leader — their cause comes to nothing; Gamaliel applies the same pragmatic test to the apostles, implicitly invoking the Deuteronomic tradition of history as the arbiter of divine vs. human origin for movements.

41.note: The suffering Servant who 'shall see the fruit of the travail of his soul and be satisfied' — the apostles rejoicing in suffering disgrace for the Name enacts the Servant's own pattern: suffering as the path to vindication; their participation in the disgrace of the Name is a share in the Servant's dishonor now revealed as honor.

Jesus's beatitude on those persecuted in his name — the apostles' rejoicing in 5:41 is the enacted beatitude; they have been insulted and persecuted for Jesus and respond with the gladness he promised. The first-person Sermon teaching now becomes third-person narrative fulfillment.

1.note: The Deuteronomic commandment to leave gleanings for the widow, orphan, and foreigner — the complaint about widows being overlooked in the daily distribution arises within the community's attempt to implement the covenant's care-for-the-vulnerable ethic; the problem is distributive fairness within the new covenant community practicing old covenant obligations.

3.note: Moses selecting seventy elders who shared in the Spirit given to him, to bear the burden of the people — the appointment of the Seven to share the apostles' administrative burden mirrors the structural solution God gave Moses in the wilderness; the Spirit-and-wisdom qualifications for the Seven parallel the Num 11 pattern of Spirit-enabled governance distributed from the founding leader.

7.note: God's eschatological promise that he would take some for priests and Levites from among the nations — the report that a large number of priests became obedient to the faith touches the leading edge of this fulfillment: the Temple priesthood beginning to cross into the new covenant community fulfills the prophetic promise of the priestly order being renewed and reconstituted.

8.note: Moses performing signs and wonders in Egypt as God's commissioned servant — Stephen doing great wonders and signs among the people presents him in the Moses-servant pattern that chapter 7's speech will develop at length; the signs authenticate Stephen as standing in the prophetic line beginning with the exodus.

11.note: False witnesses brought against Naboth to condemn him on a capital charge of blasphemy against God and the king — the false witnesses against Stephen deploy the same judicial mechanism; the pattern of judicial murder through manufactured blasphemy accusations runs from Naboth through Jesus (Matt 26:60-61) to Stephen.

14.note: Jeremiah's Temple sermon warning that God would do to the Temple what he did to Shiloh if the people did not reform — Stephen's supposed threat that Jesus 'will destroy this place' stands in the authentic prophetic tradition of Jeremiah's Temple critique; the accusation ironically charges Stephen with being prophetic in exactly the way Jeremiah was, and for the same reason.

After Jeremiah's Temple sermon the priests and prophets seized him saying 'you must die' for speaking against the Temple — the pattern of Stephen's arrest and trial precisely mirrors Jeremiah's; the same charge (threatening the Temple), the same accusers (religious authorities), the same venue (public confrontation leading to a trial).

15.note: Moses's face shining with the glory of his encounters with God on Sinai — Stephen's face appearing 'like the face of an angel' to the Sanhedrin positions him as a new Moses figure; the divine radiance that authenticated Moses's prophetic authority and frightened the Israelites now authenticates Stephen's witness before the same kind of council that resisted Moses's message.
